summary_sections.title: Understanding Atomic Structure and Subatomic Particles
summary_sections.content_html: Atoms are the fundamental units of matter, composed of a nucleus (protons and neutrons) surrounded by electron clouds. Protons are positively charged and found only in the nucleus; the number of protons defines an element (atomic number). Neutrons are neutral and found in the nucleus. Electrons are negatively charged and occupy electron clouds around the nucleus. The nucleus contains most of the atom's mass; electrons occupy most of the volume.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts and Vocabulary
summary_sections.content_html: Key terminology: atomic number (number of protons, defines element identity); mass number (protons + neutrons); isotopes (atoms of same element with different neutron numbers, creating different mass numbers); ions (atoms with unequal proton and electron numbers, giving them net charge); cations (positive ions, fewer electrons than protons); anions (negative ions, more electrons than protons). Isotopes of an element have identical chemical properties (same number of electrons) but different physical properties.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications and Examples
summary_sections.content_html: Real-world applications: the periodic table lists elements by atomic number. Carbon-12 and Carbon-14 are isotopes—C-14 is radioactive and used in carbon dating. Isotopes are used in medicine as radioisotopes for imaging and treatment. Uranium has isotopes of different masses; U-235 is fissile (undergoes fission) while U-238 is not, making isotope separation crucial for nuclear energy. Ion charges affect how atoms bond and interact in compounds.

summary_sections.title: Deeper Understanding
summary_sections.content_html: Atomic behavior depends on electron number (chemical properties), not neutron number. This is why isotopes have identical chemistry. Atoms are neutral when electron number equals proton number. Understanding atomic structure reveals why the periodic table is organized by atomic number—elements with same number of electrons in outer shells have similar chemical properties. Subatomic particle composition determines both identity (proton number) and mass.
